coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of PEG-modified fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive detection of Ag+
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Hui
Wang, Changzheng
Li, Xicheng
Han, Yuyi
Zhang, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Silver-based compounds are widely used in modern society. However, excessive use and uncontrollable discharging of silver ions (Ag+) pose a potential threat to personal safety and the environment. In this work, PEG-modified fluorescent carbon dots (PEG-CDs) were synthesized through a facile solid-state reaction, exhibiting bright blue emission with a high photoluminescence quantum yield of 73.9%. The fluorescence of as-prepared PEG-CDs can be significantly quenched by the addition Ag+, showing high selectivity and sensitivity even without interference from high concentrations of other metal ions. The fluorescence intensity decreases linearly with Ag+ from 0 μmol/L to 200 μmol/L with a limit of detection of 0.114 nmol/L.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Static quenching
Ag+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Ag+ in real samples
Detection of Ag+ in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthetic process of PEG-CDs.

(A) TEM image with the size distribution of PEG-CDs. (B) FT-IR spectroscopy of PEG-CDs, 1-pyrene-formaldehyde, o-phenylenediamine, and PEG. (C) XPS survey of PEG-CDs. (D) C1s, (E) O1s, (F) N1s high resolution XPS spectra of the PEG-CDs. (G) UV–Vis absorption spectra of PEG-CDs. (H) Excitation and emission spectra of PEG-CDs. (I) Emission spectra of PEG-CDs under different excitation wavelengths.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of PEG-CDs at different pH value, (B) buffer solution, (C) incubation time.

(A) Effect of different metal ions on the fluorescent of the PEG-CDs. (B) Fluorescence intensity of PEG-CDs quenched by Ag+ in the presence of other metal ions. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of PEG-CDs upon the addition of various concentrations of Ag+ from 0 μmol/L to 200 μmol/L (D) A linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the PEG-CDs solution and concentrations of added Ag+.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of Ag+ solution, fluorescence emission spectra and excitation spectra of PEG-CDs. (B) Fluorescence decay plot of PEG-CDs in the presence and absence of Ag+. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of PEG-CDs in the presence and absence of Ag+.
The schematic diagram to explain the fluorescence mechanism of PEG-CDs.
Comparison of other Ag + detection methods.
Detection of Ag+ in tap-water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of PEG-modified fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive detection of Ag+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Functional Materials for Building Structure and Environment Remediation, Beijing University of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Beijing, 100044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Functional Materials for Building Structure and Environment Remediation
Beijing University of Civil Engineering and Architecture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory of Functional Materials for Building Structure and Environment Remediation, Beijing University of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Beijing, 100044, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, Beijing, 100875, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silver-based compounds are widely used in modern society. However, excessive use and uncontrollable discharging of silver ions (Ag+) pose a potential threat to personal safety and the environment. In this work, PEG-modified fluorescent carbon dots (PEG-CDs) were synthesized through a facile solid-state reaction, exhibiting bright blue emission with a high photoluminescence quantum yield of 73.9%. The fluorescence of as-prepared PEG-CDs can be significantly quenched by the addition Ag+, showing high selectivity and sensitivity even without interference from high concentrations of other metal ions. The fluorescence intensity decreases linearly with Ag+ from 0 μmol/L to 200 μmol/L with a limit of detection of 0.114 nmol/L.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of silver-based compounds by mankind can be dated back to thousands of years ago. The unique antibacterial properties of silver ions (Ag+) have been of great interest for water purification, water storage, and treatment of infections [1,2]. In modern society, silver-based compounds have a broad range of applications, such as medical, cosmetic and the textile industry [3]. The widespread use of Ag+ inevitably led to their discharge into surface water bodies [4,5]. In fact, Ag+ that enter the water environment could cause different degrees of harm to aquatic organisms and pose a threat to human health and the environment [6,7]. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop a sensitive, selective and reliable approach for the detection of Ag+ in water bodies. Currently, the detection methods for Ag+ include atomic absorption spectroscopy [8,9], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [10,11], Raman spectroscopy [12], and electrochemical methods [13], and so on. Although these methods have considerable sensitivity and selectivity, the above-mentioned methods often require expensive instruments, professional operator and complicated pretreatment processes, which limited the widespread use of these methods. Recently, the fluorescence detection method for Ag+ has received increasing attention because of its advantages in simple operation, high sensitivity, time-saving and low-cost [14–16].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a relatively new type of carbon nanomaterials, have received intensive attention in recent years. The size of CDs is typically less than 10 nm, and the core structure is mainly composed of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. CDs have excellent optical properties, such as tailorable emission wavelength and stable photoluminescence so that they may have many potential applications. More importantly, CDs are biocompatible and of low toxicity, making them a promising candidate for the design of fluorescent probe. Surface passivation of CDs can effectively improve the luminescence performance of CDs and increase the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) by reducing the non-radiative recombination [17,18]. According to previous reports, PEG is one of the most widely used passivating agents due to its high water-solubility and non-toxicity. In fact, PEG can be employed not only for a passivating agent to CDs, but also a carbon source in the carbonization process, making it easier to functionalize CDs without a further surface modification process [19–21].

Herein, the blue-emissive CDs with a PLQY of 73.9% were synthesized by a one-pot, solid-state method using 1-pyrenecarboxaldehyde, o-phenylenediamine and PEG as precursors (Fig. 1 ). The as-prepared CDs can be employed as a fluorescence probe for the selective detection of Ag+ with a good linear response in the detection range of 0–200 μmol/L and the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.114 nmol/L.

All chemicals were of analytical grade. 1-pyrenecarboxaldehyde, polyethylene glycol 2,000 (PEG2000), sodium chloride, potassium chloride, aluminum chloride hexahydrate, nickel chloride hexahydrate, cobalt chloride hexahydrate, zinc chloride, manganese chloride, chromium chloride tetrahydrate, iron chloride hexahydrate, ferrous chloride tetrahydrate, mercury nitrate monohydrate, lead nitrate, tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane were procured from Innochem Technology Co., Ltd., silver nitrate were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., o-phenylenediamine were procured from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd., and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were procured from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., acetic acid, boric acid, phosphoric acid, sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory.

The morphology of CDs was investigated by JEOL JEM-2100 Transmission electron microscope (TEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with an ESCALab220i-XL electron spectrometer with 300 W Al Kα radiation. The fluorescence spectrum was measured with a FL3C-1iHR320 luminescence spectrometer with slit width at 2.0 nm. Ultraviolet visible absorption spectrum (UV–Vis) was shown by UV-2600 spectrophotometry (Shimadzu). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed on a Nicolet 380 spectrograph. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield was measured through a Varian FLR025 spectrometer with an integrating sphere.

PEG-modified CDs were synthesized by a one-pot solid-state method by using of 1-pyrenecarboxaldehyde (0.023 g, 0.1 μmol), o-phenylenediamine (0.055 g, 0.5 μmol), and PEG2000 (0.200 g, 0.1 μmol) were mixed in a ceramic mortar and ground to a fine powder. The mixture was then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene lined reactor, which was placed in an oven and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, a brown solid powder was obtained. Then, anhydrous ethanol (20 mL) was added to the polytetrafluoroethylene lined reactor, the obtained solution was subjected to sonication for 30 min. Then, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles and filtered using a 0.22 μm filtration membrane, and the filtrate was collected and freeze-dried in a freeze-drying apparatus, which was dried and prepared to a concentration of 1.2 g/L and stored at 4 °C.

A solution of PEG-CDs (2.0 mL, 0.6 g/L) and different amounts of Ag+ solution and pH = 8 Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution (1.5 mL) were added into a centrifuge tube and diluted to 4.0 mL with deionized water, and their fluorescence was measured with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm after incubation for 10 min at room temperature.

The selectivity of CDs was examined by the different metal ions such as K+, Na+, Al3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cr3+ under the identical conditions. The concentrations of Ag+ and other metal ions were all 10.0 mmol/L. Typically, different types of metal ion solutions (60.0 μL) were added into BR buffer solution (2.0 mL), and then mixed with CDs solution (2.0 mL). The corresponding fluorescence spectra at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm were quickly recorded after incubation of 10 min. In addition, to measure the fluorescence intensity of PEG-CDs when both Ag+ (20.0 μL, 10.0 mmol/L) and the above-mentioned other metal ions (100.0 μL, 10.0 mmol/L) are present to evaluate the anti-interference performance of PEG-CDs fluorescent probes. All fluorescence measurements were conducted at 293 K.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Ag+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the potential of fluorescent PEG-CDs in practical applications, the as-prepared CDs solution was employed to detect Ag+ in real water samples. Tap water samples were obtained from Xicheng District in Beijing. The collected tap water samples were centrifuged and filtered with 0.22 μm filtration membrane to remove suspended particles before the experiments. Then the fluorescence intensity of CDs was measured by adding different amounts of Ag+ to the treated water samples.

The morphology characterization of PEG-CDs was investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 (A) , PEG-CDs displayed nearly-spherical structure and dispersed with a size distribution between 1.4 and 2.6 nm. The average size of PEG-CDs is found to be 2.0 nm as shown in the particle size distribution histogram.

FT-IR and XPS measurements were carried out in order to characterize the surface structure and chemical composition of PEG-CDs. Fig. 2 (B) compared the FT-IR spectra of 1-pyrenecarboxaldehyde, o-phenylenediamine, PEG2000 and PEG-CDs. The characteristic peaks centered around 3454 and 1354 cm−1, respectively, are corresponded to stretching vibrational frequencies of the N–H/O–H and bending vibration peak of O–H groups, respectively. It can be seen that PEG-CDs contain a large number of hydroxyl groups, which may be related to the modification of CDs by PEG, proving that PEG as carbon source is involved in the synthetic process of PEG-CDs. Moreover, the characteristic peaks at 1149 and 1087 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration peaks of C–O and C–N. The peak of 1615 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration peak of CN, it is possible that the amine of o-phenylenediamine (1615 cm−1) and aldehyde groups of 1-pyreneformaldehyde underwent an amine-formaldehyde condensation reaction in the synthetic process, which is responsible for the formation of PEG-CDs. Fig. 2 (C) shows the full survey XPS spectrum of PEG-CDs with three peaks at 530.97, 397.97, and 284.97 eV for C1s (69.64%), O1s (3.63%), and N1s (2 6.73%), respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2 (D)) can be divided into four subpeaks at 284.00, 285.91, 286.70 and 285.08 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O, CO, and C–N, respectively. The O1s spectrum of PEG-CDs are shown in Fig. 2 (E), which can be divided into two main peaks at 531.95 and 532.66 eV, corresponding well to CO and C–O, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 (F), the high-resolution N1s spectrum shows two characteristic peaks at 398.3 and 399.5 eV, which is in accordance with the amino nitrogen and pyridine-like nitrogen, respectively. XPS and FT-IR characterization are consistent. The above analysis can be inferred that the surface of PEG-CDs is rich in oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups, including hydroxyl, amino, and amide groups, which are beneficial for further modification and development of PEG-CDs.

The optical properties of PEG-CDs were investigated by UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy, respectively. Fig. 2 (G) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of PEG-CDs, exhibiting two strong absorption bands at 280 and 340 nm, originating from the π-π* and n-π* transitions [22]. As demonstrated in Fig. 2 (H), the fluorescence spectrum of PEG-CDs is observed centered at 402 nm and 425 nm. When the excitation wavelengths were changed from 320 to 390 nm, the emission peak was found to be unchanged, showing the excitation-independent property (Fig. 2 (I)). The maximum emission wavelength was 402 nm. Furthermore, the PLQY of PEG-CDs was measured to be 73.9%, which is a significant increase compared to CDs without PEG functionalization. The PLQY of PEG-CDs is higher than most of previously reported CDs, indicating its potential as a highly-sensitive fluorescent probe.

Since the fluorescence of PEG-CDs can be significantly quenched in the presence of Ag+, PEG-CDs hold the great promise for the specific detection of Ag+. We explored the conditions of PEG-CDs for Ag+ detection, including the effects of pH value, buffer solution type, and incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of PEG-CDs. Fig. 3 (A) and Fig. 3 (B) show the fluorescence spectra of PEG-CDs under either different pH conditions from 4 to 10 or under different buffer solution types of Tri-HCl buffer, BR buffer, and PBS buffer was slightly influenced. Furthermore, Fig. 3 (C) shows the fluorescence intensity of PEG-CDs under different incubation times. The fluorescence intensity of PEG-CDs can be quickly quenched by 80% after adding Ag+ (80.0 μL, 10.0 mmol/L) for 1 min. Around 10 min, the fluorescence of the PEG-CDs is almost completely quenched.

To evaluate the selectivity of PEG-CDs for the detection of Ag+, the variation of fluorescence intensity of PEG-CDs in the presence of different metal ions was conducted. As shown in Fig. 4 (A) , it can be clearly observed that only Ag+ can cause significant change in PEG-CDs fluorescence, showing a high selectivity among 15 samples. The anti-interference performance of PEG-CDs for the detection of Ag+ was further investigated in the presence of different metal ions at a concentration of 250 μmol/L coexisting with Ag+. Fig. 4 (B) unveiled that the fluorescence of PEG-CDs can still be quenched by Ag+, indicating the as-synthesized PEG-CDs features good anti-interference performance in the detection of Ag+.

The sensitivity of PEG-CDs for the quantitative detection of Ag+ was systematically evaluated. Fig. 4 (C) demonstrates the fluorescence spectra of CDs after the addition of different concentrations of Ag+ from 0 to 200 μmol/L. It can be observed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases with increasing Ag + concentration. It should be noted that F0 is the fluorescence intensity of CDs without the addition of Ag + at pH = 8 in the BR buffer solution. With the optimum incubation time of 10 min, F0–F showed a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9972) with Ag+ concentration increasing to 200 μmol/L (Fig. 4 (D)), and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.114 nmol/L, the detection range of which was superior than that of previously reported CDs in the literature, while the LOD was lower than most of previous results in the literature (Table 1 ), highlighting an excellent detection capability of PEG-CDs. In addition, compared with the reported CDs for pollutant detection, PEG CDs also show high PLQY [23–29].

In order to establish the mechanism of the specific detection Ag+ by PEG-CDs, the absorption spectra of Ag+ and the excitation and emission spectra of PEG-CDs are characterized. As shown in Fig. 5 (A) , the absorption spectra of Ag+ does not overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of PEG-CDs. Therefore, the quenching of PEG-CDs by Ag+ can exclude the internal filtration effect. The fluorescence lifetime of PEG-CDs before and after quenching was then investigated. Fig. 5 (B) shows the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of PEG-CDs before and after the addition of Ag+. The average fluorescence lifetime of PEG-CDs was calculated to be 2.06 ns, which was fitted using a single exponential function. With the addition of Ag+, the fluorescence lifetime of the mixed solution was 1.95 ns. No considerable change in their fluorescence lifetime was observed before and after the addition of Ag+, indicating that the major quenching mechanism would be static quenching (SQE).

The quenching mechanism is further investigated based on the Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
/ F
= 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of PEG-CDs and PEG-CDs/Ag+ when excited at 340 nm excitation wavelength, respectively, K sν is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, Q is the concentration of Ag+, K q is the bimolecular quenching constant, and τ 0 is the average fluorescence lifetime of the CDs, which is 2.06 ns. Based on the calculation, K sν = 14939.64 L/mol, K q = 7.25 × 1012 L mol−1 s−1, K q is larger than the previously reported dynamic quenching constant 1.0-2.0 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1, showing that the fluorescence quenching of PEG-CDs is mainly due to SQE [48–51].

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of PEG-CDs, PEG-CDs/Ag+ solutions were compared in Fig. 5 (C), demonstrating the difference before and after the addition of Ag+. In particular, a new absorption peak appeared at 443 nm after the addition of Ag+ while the absorption intensity at 344 nm was found to be largely weakened, indicating the formation of complex by PEG-CDs and added Ag+, further verifying that the above-mentioned detection mechanism for Ag+ by PEG-CDs is SQE (Fig. 6 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the utility of the PEG-CDs fluorescent probe, pretreated tap water samples were used to prepare silver ion solutions for simulating real samples. As shown in Table 2 , high recoveries of 96.75%–102.96% were achieved with RSD (n = 3) less than 6%. The result demonstrates the potential of PEG-CDs for the detection of Ag+ in practical applications.

In summary, PEG-CDs were synthesized by a facile solid-state approach, exhibiting a bright blue fluorescence with a high PLQY up to 73.9%. PEG-CDs can be developed as a fluorescent probe due to its good selectivity and anti-interference ability, exhibiting a high sensitivity for Ag+ detection with a LOD as low as 0.114 nmol/L in the wide detection range of 0–200 μmol/L, and good linear relationship between Ag+ concentration and fluorescence intensity. The quenching mechanism for the detection of Ag+ can be ascribe to the static quenching. Most importantly, PEG-CDs can be utilized to for the analysis of Ag+ in tap water samples. Therefore, PEG-CDs hold the promise for the detection of Ag+ in water samples.

Hui Li: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Changzheng Wang: Designed the experiment, Writing – review & editing. Xicheng Li: Validation, Formal analysis. Yuyi Han: Validation, Formal analysis. Yang Zhang: Designed the experiment, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21872010, 22172008), Fundamental Research Funds for Beijing University of Civil Engineering and Architecture (No. X18005, X18279), and BUCEA Post Graduate Innovation Project (No. DG2023012, PG2023076).
